course_identity.notes: Matched CSC 212 (Data Structures) Spring 2025 offering; instructor Marco Alvarez. Syllabus uploaded PDF used as ground truth.

grading_categories.name: Homework
grading_categories.notes: Individual unless stated; autograded via Gradescope; new assignment roughly every 6–9 days.

grading_categories.name: Exams
grading_categories.notes: Three exams: Midterm 1, Midterm 2, Final. Open-book (printed/handwritten notes only); no electronic devices.

grading_categories.name: Participation / Labs
grading_categories.notes: Weekly lab attendance required for participation credit; syllabus lists 5–10% extra credit tied to participation (not part of the 35/65 split above).

course_profile.late_policy: No late submissions accepted for assignments.
course_profile.attendance_policy: Lab sessions are structured (lecture segments, live coding, exercises); attendance expected per syllabus.
course_profile.drop_rules: Follow URI academic calendar and department policies for drops/withdrawals.
required_tools: C++ development environment (course-specific toolchain per instructor)
course_profile.course_summary: CSC 212 covers fundamental data structures and algorithms—lists, stacks, queues, trees, graphs, and hash tables—with implementation emphasis in C++ and analysis using asymptotic notation.
course_profile.workflow_notes: Lectures TR 9:30–10:45 AM; multiple Wednesday lab sections. Prerequisites: CSC 211 (C- or better) and MTH 180 or Computer Engineering major status. Office hours location noted as Tyler Hall CS lounge (schedule TBD). Academic integrity policy prohibits uncredited AI use; AI is allowed if cited and you understand all submitted work. Support: AEC; disability accommodations via DAI; religious observances require advance written notice.

resources.title: URI Computer Science — department hub
resources.reason: Official CS department page for policies, labs, and advising context.

resources.title: VisuAlgo — data structures visualizations
resources.reason: Interactive visualizations for trees, graphs, heaps, and sorting—pairs well with CSC 212 topics.

resources.title: GeeksforGeeks — Data Structures
resources.reason: Quick reference for implementations and interview-style explanations.

resources.title: OpenDSA — interactive textbook modules
resources.reason: Supplemental practice aligned with undergraduate DS curricula.

detected_tools.evidence: Listed in syllabus as platform for discussion, polls, quizzes, and collaboration.
Syllabus specifies assignment submission and autograding through Gradescope.
Syllabus references virtual meetings and support sessions.

student_signal.workload: Moderate to high — steady coding assignments on a 6–9 day cadence plus three exams.
student_signal.difficulty: Typical second-year CS data structures pace; pointer-heavy and proof-style analysis on exams.
student_signal.grading_style: Strict no-late policy on homework; majority of grade from exams (65%).
key_warnings: Exams prohibit electronic devices; open notes only if printed/handwritten
Academic integrity explicitly calls out uncredited AI use
positive_signals: Multiple lab section times on Wednesdays
AI use permitted with citation and understanding requirement
Clear support pathways (AEC, DAI, religious observance process)
student_signal.summary: Structured, exam-weighted course with firm deadlines. Strong fit if you keep up weekly and budget time for open-book exam prep without relying on devices.

obligations.title: Final Exam
obligations.conflict_note: Confirm exact slot with URI final exam schedule.
